lem:clip-balance-exponent.proof_tex:
A number \(z\) is attainable at some \(s\ge0\) iff
\[
z\le A(1-s),
\qquad z\le c-s,
\qquad z\le a+\frac{s}{2\gamma}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\max\{0,2\gamma(z-a)\}\le \min\{1-z/A,c-z\}. \tag{1}
\]
Condition (1) first forces \(z\le A\) and \(z\le c\). If \(z>a\), it also forces
\[
2\gamma(z-a)\le 1-z/A,
\qquad
2\gamma(z-a)\le c-z,
\]
that is
\[
z\le \frac{A(1+2\gamma a)}{1+2\gamma A},
\qquad
z\le \frac{c+2\gamma a}{1+2\gamma}.
\]
If \(z\le a\), the lower bound in (1) is zero, so no additional restriction beyond \(z\le A\) and \(z\le c\) is needed; these restrictions are already included in the displayed minimum. Thus every attainable value is at most
\[
\bar g=\min\left\{A,c,\frac{A(1+2\gamma a)}{1+2\gamma A},\frac{c+2\gamma a}{1+2\gamma}\right\}.
\]
Conversely set \(s_\star=\max\{0,2\gamma(\bar g-a)\}\). The four inequalities defining \(\bar g\) are exactly the inequalities needed for
\[
s_\star\le1-\bar g/A,
\qquad s_\star\le c-
\bar g,
\qquad s_\star\ge2\gamma(
\bar g-a),
\]
with the last one automatic by definition. Hence all three affine terms are at least \(\bar g\) at \(s_\star\), so the supremum is at least \(\bar g\). The upper and lower bounds agree, proving the formula and the optimizer claim.

snapshot.stmt: For gamma>0, A in (0,1], a>=0, and c>=0, define g(A,a,c,gamma)=sup_{s>=0} min{A(1-s), c-s, a+s/(2 gamma)}. Then g=min{A, c, A(1+2 gamma a)/(1+2 gamma A), (c+2 gamma a)/(1+2 gamma)}. One optimizer is s_star=max{0,2 gamma (g-a)}.

node.statement: For gamma>0, A in (0,1], a>=0, and c>=0, define g(A,a,c,gamma)=sup_{s>=0} min{A(1-s), c-s, a+s/(2 gamma)}. Then g=min{A, c, A(1+2 gamma a)/(1+2 gamma A), (c+2 gamma a)/(1+2 gamma)}. One optimizer is s_star=max{0,2 gamma (g-a)}.
node.proof_tex:
A number \(z\) is attainable at some \(s\ge0\) iff
\[
z\le A(1-s),
\qquad z\le c-s,
\qquad z\le a+\frac{s}{2\gamma}.

\[

\]

\[
2\gamma(z-a)\le 1-z/A,
\qquad
2\gamma(z-a)\le c-z,
\]
that is
\[
z\le \frac{A(1+2\gamma a)}{1+2\gamma A},
\qquad
z\le \frac{c+2\gamma a}{1+2\gamma}.
\]
If \(z\le a\), the lower bound in (1) is zero, so no additional restriction beyond \(z\le A\) and \(z\le c\) is needed; these restrictions are already included in the displayed minimum. Thus every attainable value is at most
\[
\bar g=\min\left\{A,c,\frac{A(1+2\gamma a)}{1+2\gamma A},\frac{c+2\gamma a}{1+2\gamma}\right\}.
\]
Conversely set \(s_\star=\max\{0,2\gamma(\bar g-a)\}\). The four inequalities defining \(\bar g\) are exactly the inequalities needed for
\[
s_\star\le1-\bar g/A,
\qquad s_\star\le c-
\bar g,
\qquad s_\star\ge2\gamma(
\bar g-a),
\]
with the last one automatic by definition. Hence all three affine terms are at least \(\bar g\) at \(s_\star\), so the supremum is at least \(\bar g\). The upper and lower bounds agree, proving the formula and the optimizer claim.

lem:clipped-score-variance.proof_tex:
Condition on the training folds, so the fitted nuisance functions are fixed on the evaluation observation. By ass:bounded-outcome and ass:bounded-crossfit-nuisances, \(|Y-\widehat\mu_a(X)|\le2\). Given \(X=x\), the only random terms in the score are the two inverse-propensity residual terms. Therefore, for a universal constant \(C\),
\[
\operatorname{Var}_P\{\Gamma_q(O;\widehat\eta)\mid X=x\}
\le C\left\{\frac{e_P(x)}{\widehat e_q(x)^2}+\frac{1-e_P(x)}{(1-\widehat e_q(x))^2}\right\}.
\]
The proposed variance-stability assumption bounds the brace by \(C_v/\max\{p_P(x),q\}\). Since \(\max\{p_P(x),q\}\ge q\), the second inequality follows.

snapshot.stmt: Under ass:bounded-outcome, ass:bounded-crossfit-nuisances, and ass:clipped-propensity-variance-stability, the cross-fitted clipped AIPW score satisfies Var_P(Gamma_q(O; etahat) | X=x) <= C/max{p_P(x),q} <= C/q conditionally on the training folds.

defs.def:clipped-aipw-score: Gamma_q(O; eta) = mu_1(X) - mu_0(X) + (A/e_q(X;eta))(Y - mu_1(X)) - ((1-A)/(1-e_q(X;eta)))(Y - mu_0(X)).

assumptions.ass:bounded-crossfit-nuisances: The cross-fitted outcome-regression estimates muhat_0, muhat_1 take values in [-1,1].

node.statement: Under ass:bounded-outcome, ass:bounded-crossfit-nuisances, and ass:clipped-propensity-variance-stability, the cross-fitted clipped AIPW score satisfies Var_P(Gamma_q(O; etahat) | X=x) <= C/max{p_P(x),q} <= C/q conditionally on the training folds.
node.proof_tex:
Condition on the training folds, so the fitted nuisance functions are fixed on the evaluation observation. By ass:bounded-outcome and ass:bounded-crossfit-nuisances, \(|Y-\widehat\mu_a(X)|\le2\). Given \(X=x\), the only random terms in the score are the two inverse-propensity residual terms. Therefore, for a universal constant \(C\),
\[
\operatorname{Var}_P\{\Gamma_q(O;\widehat\eta)\mid X=x\}
\le C\left\{\frac{e_P(x)}{\widehat e_q(x)^2}+\frac{1-e_P(x)}{(1-\widehat e_q(x))^2}\right\}.
\]
The proposed variance-stability assumption bounds the brace by \(C_v/\max\{p_P(x),q\}\). Since \(\max\{p_P(x),q\}\ge q\), the second inequality follows.

thm:welfare-identity.proof_tex:
By def:welfare-regret,
\[
R_P(\pi)=V_P(\pi_\star)-V_P(\pi)=E_P[(\pi_\star(X)-\pi(X))\tau_P(X)].
\]
The integrand is integrable because ass:bounded-outcome gives \(\mu_a(X)\in[-1,1]\) and hence \(|\tau_P(X)|\le 2\). Fix \(x\). If \(\tau_P(x)>0\), then \(\pi_\star(x)=1\), so
\[
(\pi_\star(x)-\pi(x))\tau_P(x)=(1-\pi(x))\tau_P(x)=|\tau_P(x)|1\{\pi(x)\ne \pi_\star(x)\}.
\]
If \(\tau_P(x)<0\), then \(\pi_\star(x)=0\), so
\[
(\pi_\star(x)-\pi(x))\tau_P(x)=-\pi(x)\tau_P(x)=|\tau_P(x)|1\{\pi(x)\ne \pi_\star(x)\}.
\]
If \(\tau_P(x)=0\), both sides are zero, regardless of the value of \(\pi(x)\). Therefore the equality holds pointwise. Taking expectation and then disintegrating over \(P_X\) gives
\[
R_P(\pi)=E_P\{|\tau_P(X)|1(\pi(X)\ne\pi_\star(X))\}
=\int_X |\tau_P(x)|1\{\pi(x)\ne\pi_\star(x)\}\,dP_X(x).
\]
The i.i.d. sampling assumption is not needed for the identity itself; it only fixes the sampling model for later estimator statements.

snapshot.stmt: Under ass:iid and ass:bounded-outcome, for every deterministic pi in Pi, R_P(pi) = E_P[ |tau_P(X)| 1{pi(X) != pi_star(X)} ] = integral over X of |tau_P(x)| 1{pi(x) != pi_star(x)} dP_X(x).

defs.def:welfare-regret: V_P(pi) = E_P[pi(X) tau_P(X)]; pi_star(x) = 1{tau_P(x) >= 0}; R_P(pi) = V_P(pi_star) - V_P(pi).

thm:margin-localization.proof_tex:
Let \(D_\pi=\{x:\pi(x)\ne\pi_\star(x)\}\). By ass:zero-effect, \(P_X(D_\pi\cap\{\tau_P=0\})=0\): either the zero-effect set has zero \(P_X\)-mass, or every policy in \(\Pi\) agrees with \(\pi_\star\) there. If \(R_P(\pi)=0\), thm:welfare-identity gives
\[
\int_{D_\pi}|\tau_P(x)|\,dP_X(x)=0.
\]
Since the integrand is nonnegative and the zero-effect part of \(D_\pi\) has zero mass, \(P_X(D_\pi)=0\), and the desired bound is trivial.

Assume now \(R=R_P(\pi)>0\). For any \(t\in(0,u_0]\),
\[
P_X(D_\pi)\le P_X(D_\pi\cap\{0<|\tau_P|\le t\})+P_X(D_\pi\cap\{|\tau_P|>t\}).
\]
By ass:margin the first term is at most \(C_m t^\alpha\). By thm:welfare-identity, on \(D_\pi\cap\{|\tau_P|>t\}\) the regret integrand is at least \(t\), so the second term is at most \(R/t\). Hence
\[
P_X(D_\pi)\le C_m t^\alpha+R/t,\qquad 0<t\le u_0. \tag{1}
\]
If \(0<R\le C_m u_0^{1+\alpha}\), take \(t=(R/C_m)^{1/(1+\alpha)}\le u_0\) in (1). Then
\[
P_X(D_\pi)\le 2C_m^{1/(1+\alpha)}R^{\alpha/(1+\alpha)}.
\]
If \(R>C_m u_0^{1+\alpha}\), then
\[
P_X(D_\pi)\le 1\le (C_m u_0^{1+\alpha})^{-\alpha/(1+\alpha)}R^{\alpha/(1+\alpha)}
\]
when \(\alpha>0\); for \(\alpha=0\) the bound is simply \(P_X(D_\pi)\le1\). Combining the cases gives
\[
P_X(D_\pi)\le C(C_m,u_0,\alpha)R_P(\pi)^{\alpha/(1+\alpha)},
\]
with the zero-regret case handled separately when \(\alpha=0\).

snapshot.stmt: Under ass:margin and ass:zero-effect there is C=C(C_m,u_0,alpha) such that for every pi in Pi, P_X(D_pi) <= C R_P(pi)^(alpha/(1+alpha)).

assumptions.ass:margin: P(0 < |tau_P(X)| <= u) <= C_m u^alpha for all 0 < u <= u_0.
assumptions.ass:zero-effect: Either P_X{tau_P(X)=0}=0, or every pi in Pi agrees with pi_star P_X-almost everywhere on {tau_P(X)=0}.

prop:overlap-envelope.proof_tex:
For \(\gamma>0\), put \(v=h^\beta\) and \(u=h^{\beta/\gamma}\). Then
\[
u^\alpha v^{1/\gamma}=(h^{\beta/\gamma})^\alpha(h^\beta)^{1/\gamma}
=h^{(\alpha+1)\beta/\gamma}.
\]
Because \(0<h<1\), \(h^a\ge h^b\) holds exactly when \(a\le b\). Therefore
\[
u^\alpha v^{1/\gamma}\ge h^\alpha
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\frac{(\alpha+1)\beta}{\gamma}\le\alpha
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\beta\le\frac{\alpha\gamma}{\alpha+1}=\beta_{\alpha,\gamma}.
\]
Equality holds at \(\beta=\beta_{\alpha,\gamma}\), by def:exponents.

Now consider a block of mass comparable to \(h^\alpha\), contrast comparable to \(h\), and weak-arm propensity comparable to \(h^\beta\). The overlap-decay condition can see the block only for windows with \(u\gtrsim h\), \(v\gtrsim h^\beta\), and \(v\le c_o u^\gamma\). If \(\beta\le\gamma\), the least favorable such window, up to constants, is exactly \(v=h^\beta\), \(u=h^{\beta/\gamma}\), so the preceding display says that the envelope permits mass \(h^\alpha\) exactly when \(\beta\le\beta_{\alpha,\gamma}\). If \(\beta>\gamma\), the smallest admissible contrast window is instead \(u=h\); then the right side of ass:overlap-decay is of order
\[
h^\alpha h^{\beta/\gamma},
\]
which is strictly smaller than \(h^\alpha\), so the block cannot be covered. Since \(\beta_{\alpha,\gamma}=\alpha\gamma/(1+\alpha)\le\gamma\), the two cases together give the asserted iff. Larger \(\beta\) means a smaller weak-arm propensity and less information; hence \(\beta_{\alpha,\gamma}\) is the maximal, i.e. least informative, admissible weak-arm exponent.

snapshot.stmt: For alpha >= 0, gamma > 0, h in (0,1), beta >= 0, at the tight window v=h^beta, u=h^(beta/gamma): u^alpha v^(1/gamma) = h^((alpha+1)beta/gamma), and u^alpha v^(1/gamma) >= h^alpha iff beta <= alpha gamma/(alpha+1), with equality at beta = beta_ag. Hence a block of mass ~ h^alpha, contrast ~ h, propensity ~ h^beta meets the ass:overlap-decay envelope iff beta <= beta_ag, and beta_ag is the least informative admissible weak-arm exponent.

defs.def:exponents: beta_ag = (0 if gamma = 0 else alpha gamma/(alpha+1)); D_ag = 2 + alpha + beta_ag; r_star(alpha,gamma) = (1+alpha)/D_ag.

assumptions.ass:overlap-decay: P{p_P(X) <= v, 0 < |tau_P(X)| <= u} <= C_o u^alpha v^(1/gamma) for all 0 < v <= c_o u^gamma, with v^(1/gamma)=1 when gamma=0.

lem:witness-membership.proof_tex:
Read def:two-point-witness as the displayed two-point outcome law on \(\{-1,1\}\) on the active treated cell, so that \(P(Y(1)=1\mid X\in B_n)=(1+\sigma h_n)/2\) and \(E[Y(1)\mid X\in B_n]=\sigma h_n\). Thus \(X\sim\mathrm{Unif}[0,1]\), \(B_n=[0,c_Bh_n^\alpha]\), \(m_n=P_X(B_n)=c_Bh_n^\alpha\), the propensity is \(q_n\) on \(B_n\) and \(1/2\) on \(B_n^c\), and the active-block contrast is \(\tau_{P_{n,\sigma}}=\sigma h_n\). Off the block the common contrast is some \(\tau_0\in(u_0,2)\), which exists by ass:margin-window.

Sampling is i.i.d. by construction. Consistency and unconfoundedness are obtained by first drawing potential outcomes from their conditional laws given \(X\), then drawing \(A\) from the displayed propensity independently of the potential outcomes given \(X\), and finally setting \(Y=AY(1)+(1-A)Y(0)\). The outcome support is contained in \([-1,1]\), so ass:bounded-outcome holds. Positivity holds because \(q_n>0\) and, for all large \(n\), \(q_n\le1/2\), while the off-block propensity is \(1/2\). The contrast is never zero for all sufficiently large \(n\), so ass:zero-effect holds.

For the margin condition, fix \(0<u\le u_0\). Since the off-block contrast is \(\tau_0>u_0\), the event \(0<|\tau|\le u\) can occur only on \(B_n\). If \(u<h_n\), the event is empty. If \(h_n\le u\le u_0\), its mass is \(m_n=c_Bh_n^\alpha\le c_Bu^\alpha\). Choosing \(c_B\le C_m\) gives ass:margin.

Next check ass:overlap-decay. When \(\gamma=0\), the convention is \(v^{1/\gamma}=1\) and \(q_n=1/4\). If \(v<q_n\), the event \(\{p_P\le v,0<|\tau|\le u\}\) is empty. If \(v\ge q_n\) and \(u\ge h_n\), its mass is \(m_n=c_Bh_n^\alpha\le c_Bu^\alpha\), so the envelope holds after taking \(c_B\le C_o\). When \(\gamma>0\), put \(\beta=\beta_{\alpha,\gamma}\). If \(\beta>0\), then \(q_n=h_n^\beta\); if \(\beta=0\), then \(q_n\) is the fixed value \(1/4\). The event is empty unless \(u\ge h_n\) and \(v\ge q_n\). Under the side condition \(v\le c_ou^\gamma\),
\[
u^\alpha v^{1/\gamma}\ge c_o^{-\alpha/\gamma}v^{(\alpha+1)/\gamma}.
\]
For \(\beta>0\), the last display is at least a constant multiple of \(q_n^{(\alpha+1)/\gamma}=h_n^\alpha\), by prop:overlap-envelope at \(\beta=\beta_{\alpha,\gamma}\). For \(\beta=0\), it is bounded below by a positive constant whenever the event is nonempty, while \(m_n=c_B\). In both cases \(m_n\le C_ou^\alpha v^{1/\gamma}\) follows by choosing \(c_B\) small enough relative to the fixed envelope constants. Hence ass:overlap-decay holds.

The enlarged def:law-class also contains ass:marginal-overlap and ass:strict-overlap-endpoint. For ass:marginal-overlap, if \(\gamma=0\), then \(P_X\{p_P\le v\}\le1=C_{om}v^{1/\gamma}\) after taking \(C_{om}\ge1\) under the stated convention. If \(\gamma>0\), then for \(v<q_n\) the lower-propensity event is empty on \(B_n\); for \(q_n\le v<1/2\) it has mass \(m_n=c_Bh_n^\alpha\). Since \(q_n=h_n^{\beta_{\alpha,\gamma}}\) when \(\beta_{\alpha,\gamma}>0\),
\[
m_n=c_Bh_n^\alpha\le C h_n^{\beta_{\alpha,\gamma}/\gamma}=C q_n^{1/\gamma}\le C v^{1/\gamma},
\]
because \(\alpha\ge \beta_{\alpha,\gamma}/\gamma=\alpha/(1+\alpha)\) and \(0<h_n<1\). If \(\beta_{\alpha,\gamma}=0\), then \(m_n=c_B\) and the inequality is again a fixed-constant bound for \(v\ge q_n=1/4\). For \(v\ge1/2\), the event has mass at most one and is covered by enlarging \(C_{om}\). Thus ass:marginal-overlap holds. Ass:strict-overlap-endpoint is vacuous for \(\gamma>0\); for \(\gamma=0\), the witness has \(p_P(x)\in\{1/4,1/2\}\), so it holds with \(\underline p=1/4\).

The optimal policy under \(P_{n,+}\) is \(x\mapsto1\); under \(P_{n,-}\) it is \(x\mapsto1\{x\notin B_n\}\). The standing ass:policy-class supplies finite VC measurability/rank for \(\Pi\), and the additional richness hypothesis in the statement puts these two optimal policies in \(\Pi\), giving ass:optimal-in-class for the witness laws. Combining all verified member properties with def:law-class gives \(P_{n,+},P_{n,-}\in\mathcal P_{\alpha,\gamma}\) for all sufficiently large \(n\).

snapshot.stmt: For all large n the corrected witness laws P_{n,+}, P_{n,-} satisfy ass:iid, ass:consistency, ass:unconfoundedness, ass:bounded-outcome, ass:positivity, ass:margin, ass:zero-effect, and ass:overlap-decay. If, in addition, the two explicit witness-optimal policies x -> 1 and x -> 1{x notin B_n} belong to Pi, then P_{n,+}, P_{n,-} lie in def:law-class P_class(alpha,gamma). (Normalization: the margin window satisfies u_0<2, so an off-block contrast tau_0 in (u_0, 2) exists under bounded outcomes |Y|<=1.)

defs.def:two-point-witness: On X=[0,1] with P_X=Lebesgue, fix h_n=n^{-1/(2+alpha+beta_ag)}, q_n=(1/4 if beta_ag=0 else h_n^{beta_ag}), and an active block B_n=[0, c_B h_n^alpha] with P_X(B_n)=c_B h_n^alpha. The two laws P_{n,sigma}, sigma in {+,-}, share P_X, share the logging propensity e(x)=q_n on B_n and e(x)=1/2 off B_n, and share an off-block outcome law with common optimal label 1 and contrast tau_0 > u_0. On B_n set mu_0(x)=0 and let the treated outcome on the active treated cell {X in B_n, A=1} satisfy P(Y=1 | X in B_n, A=1)=(1+sigma h_n)/2, so tau_{P_{n,sigma}}=sigma h_n on B_n. The laws agree everywhere off the treated active cell. Constants are chosen with 8 C_B C_Q < log 5.
defs.def:law-class: { P : ass:iid, ass:consistency, ass:unconfoundedness, ass:bounded-outcome, ass:positivity, ass:margin, ass:zero-effect, ass:overlap-decay, ass:marginal-overlap, ass:strict-overlap-endpoint, ass:policy-class, and ass:optimal-in-class hold at exponents (alpha,gamma) }

assumptions.ass:overlap-decay: P{p_P(X) <= v, 0 < |tau_P(X)| <= u} <= C_o u^alpha v^(1/gamma) for all 0 < v <= c_o u^gamma, with v^(1/gamma)=1 when gamma=0.
assumptions.ass:margin: P(0 < |tau_P(X)| <= u) <= C_m u^alpha for all 0 < u <= u_0.
assumptions.ass:iid: O_1, ..., O_n are i.i.d. draws from P.
assumptions.ass:unconfoundedness: (Y(0), Y(1)) is independent of A given X under P.

lem:two-point-divergence.proof_tex:
The two witness laws have the same covariate marginal, the same propensity, and the same conditional outcome law except on the active treated cell \(B_n\times\{A=1\}\). This cell has probability \(m_nq_n\) under both laws. Conditional on the cell, the outcome is Rademacher with success probabilities \((1+h_n)/2\) under \(P_{n,+}\) and \((1-h_n)/2\) under \(P_{n,-}\). For Bernoulli laws with probabilities \(p=(1+h)/2\) and \(r=(1-h)/2\),
\[
\chi^2(\mathrm{Bern}(p)\|\mathrm{Bern}(r))
=(p-r)^2\left(\frac1r+\frac1{1-r}\right)
=\frac{4h^2}{1-h^2}\le 8h^2
\]
for all sufficiently small \(h\). Since the likelihood ratio is one off the active treated cell,
\[
\chi^2(P_{n,+}\|P_{n,-})\le C m_n q_n h_n^2.
\]
By construction \(m_n\asymp h_n^\alpha\) and \(q_n\asymp h_n^{\beta_{\alpha,\gamma}}\), where \(q_n\) is fixed when \(\beta_{\alpha,\gamma}=0\). Thus
\[
\chi^2(P_{n,+}\|P_{n,-})\le C h_n^{2+\alpha+\beta_{\alpha,\gamma}}.
\]
For product measures, \(1+\chi^2(P^{\otimes n}\|Q^{\otimes n})=(1+\chi^2(P\|Q))^n\). With \(h_n=n^{-1/(2+\alpha+\beta_{\alpha,\gamma})}\),
\[
n h_n^{2+\alpha+\beta_{\alpha,\gamma}}=1,
\]
and therefore
\[
1+\chi^2(P_{n,+}^{\otimes n}\|P_{n,-}^{\otimes n})
\le (1+C/n)^n\le e^C.
\]
Equivalently, the \(n\)-fold chi-square divergence is bounded by a finite constant independent of \(n\).

snapshot.stmt: For the def:two-point-witness laws, the per-observation chi-square is chi^2(P_{n,+} || P_{n,-}) <= C m_n q_n h_n^2 with m_n=P_X(B_n)~h_n^alpha and q_n~h_n^{beta_ag}, hence ~ h_n^{2+alpha+beta_ag}; for h_n=n^{-1/(2+alpha+beta_ag)} the n-fold divergence is bounded, chi^2(P_{n,+}^{⊗n} || P_{n,-}^{⊗n}) <= C (equivalently 1+chi^2(P^{⊗n}) = (1+O(h_n^{2+alpha+beta_ag}))^n <= e^{C}).

defs.def:two-point-witness: On X=[0,1] with P_X=Lebesgue, fix h_n=n^{-1/(2+alpha+beta_ag)}, q_n=(1/4 if beta_ag=0 else h_n^{beta_ag}), and an active block B_n=[0, c_B h_n^alpha] with P_X(B_n)=c_B h_n^alpha. The two laws P_{n,sigma}, sigma in {+,-}, share P_X, share the logging propensity e(x)=q_n on B_n and e(x)=1/2 off B_n, and share an off-block outcome law with common optimal label 1 and contrast tau_0 > u_0. On B_n set mu_0(x)=0 and let the treated outcome on the active treated cell {X in B_n, A=1} satisfy P(Y=1 | X in B_n, A=1)=(1+sigma h_n)/2, so tau_{P_{n,sigma}}=sigma h_n on B_n. The laws agree everywhere off the treated active cell. Constants are chosen with 8 C_B C_Q < log 5.
defs.def:exponents: beta_ag = (0 if gamma = 0 else alpha gamma/(alpha+1)); D_ag = 2 + alpha + beta_ag; r_star(alpha,gamma) = (1+alpha)/D_ag.

lem:regret-separation.proof_tex:
On \(B_n\), the corrected witness has \(\tau_{P_{n,+}}=h_n\) and \(\tau_{P_{n,-}}=-h_n\). Hence \(\pi_{\star,+}=1\) on \(B_n\), while \(\pi_{\star,-}=0\) on \(B_n\). For any deterministic \(\pi\), at each \(x\in B_n\) exactly one of the two indicators \(1\{\pi(x)\ne\pi_{\star,+}(x)\}\) and \(1\{\pi(x)\ne\pi_{\star,-}(x)\}\) equals one. By thm:welfare-identity,
\[
R_{P_{n,+}}(\pi)+R_{P_{n,-}}(\pi)
\ge h_n\int_{B_n}\{1\{\pi(x)\ne1\}+1\{\pi(x)\ne0\}\}\,dP_X(x)
=h_nP_X(B_n).
\]
Therefore
\[
\max_{\sigma\in\{+,-\}}R_{P_{n,\sigma}}(\pi)
\ge \frac12 h_nP_X(B_n)
=\frac{c_B}{2}h_n^{1+\alpha}.
\]
Taking the infimum over \(\pi\in\Pi\) gives the claimed separation.

snapshot.stmt: For the def:two-point-witness laws, inf_{pi in Pi} max_{sigma in {+,-}} R_{P_{n,sigma}}(pi) >= c h_n^(1+alpha): the optimal policies are opposite on B_n, so any pi misclassifies B_n under at least one law and pays regret >= (1/2) P_X(B_n) h_n ~ h_n^(1+alpha).

defs.def:two-point-witness: On X=[0,1] with P_X=Lebesgue, fix h_n=n^{-1/(2+alpha+beta_ag)}, q_n=(1/4 if beta_ag=0 else h_n^{beta_ag}), and an active block B_n=[0, c_B h_n^alpha] with P_X(B_n)=c_B h_n^alpha. The two laws P_{n,sigma}, sigma in {+,-}, share P_X, share the logging propensity e(x)=q_n on B_n and e(x)=1/2 off B_n, and share an off-block outcome law with common optimal label 1 and contrast tau_0 > u_0. On B_n set mu_0(x)=0 and let the treated outcome on the active treated cell {X in B_n, A=1} satisfy P(Y=1 | X in B_n, A=1)=(1+sigma h_n)/2, so tau_{P_{n,sigma}}=sigma h_n on B_n. The laws agree everywhere off the treated active cell. Constants are chosen with 8 C_B C_Q < log 5.

lem:le-cam-two-point-chisq.proof_tex:
This is a cited classical substrate result. Tsybakov, Introduction to Nonparametric Estimation, 2009, Chapter 2, Theorem 2.2 states the Le Cam two-point reduction in terms of the total-variation testing affinity, and the Bretagnolle--Huber inequality gives, for any test \(\psi\),
\[
P_+^{\otimes n}(\psi\ne +)+P_-^{\otimes n}(\psi\ne -)
\ge \frac12\exp\{-\mathrm{KL}(P_+^{\otimes n},P_-^{\otimes n})\}.
\]
Since \(\mathrm{KL}(P_+,P_-)\le\chi^2(P_+\|P_-)\le c_0/n\) and KL tensorizes for product measures, \(\mathrm{KL}(P_+^{\otimes n},P_-^{\otimes n})\le c_0\). Therefore every test has summed error at least \(c_1=(1/2)e^{-c_0}>0\). Equivalently, using Tsybakov's total-variation form and \(\mathrm{TV}(P,Q)\le\{\chi^2(P\|Q)/(1+\chi^2(P\|Q))\}^{1/2}\) gives the same positive constant whenever the product chi-square is bounded. If a decision rule incurs loss at least \(\Delta\) whenever it selects the wrong member of the two-point pair, this testing lower bound gives minimax risk at least a constant multiple of \(\Delta\).

snapshot.stmt: Le Cam two-point testing bound. For product laws P_+^{\otimes n}, P_-^{\otimes n} with per-observation chi-square chi^2(P_+ || P_-) <= c_0/n, every test psi satisfies inf_psi [P_+^{\otimes n}(psi != +) + P_-^{\otimes n}(psi != -)] >= c_1 > 0 for a constant c_1=c_1(c_0); combined with a parameter separation this yields the two-point minimax lower bound.

thm:minimax-lower.proof_tex:
Let \(P_+=P_{n,+}\), \(P_-=P_{n,-}\), and let \(\widehat\pi\) be any measurable \(\Pi\)-valued estimator. By lem:witness-membership, both laws belong to \(\mathcal P_{\alpha,\gamma}\) for all large \(n\). Thus
\[
\sup_{P\in\mathcal P_{\alpha,\gamma}}E_PR_P(\widehat\pi)
\ge \max\{E_{P_+}R_{P_+}(\widehat\pi),E_{P_-}R_{P_-}(\widehat\pi)\}.
\]
For each \(x\in B_n\), the decision \(\widehat\pi(x)\) is a test between \(P_+^{\otimes n}\) and \(P_-^{\otimes n}\): choosing label \(1\) is correct under \(P_+\) and choosing label \(0\) is correct under \(P_-\). Therefore
\[
E_{P_+}R_{P_+}(\widehat\pi)+E_{P_-}R_{P_-}(\widehat\pi)
\ge h_n\int_{B_n}\{P_+^{\otimes n}(\widehat\pi(x)=0)+P_-^{\otimes n}(\widehat\pi(x)=1)\}\,dP_X(x).
\]
By lem:le-cam-two-point-chisq and lem:two-point-divergence, the testing-error term in braces is bounded below by a positive constant \(c_0\) independent of \(n\). Hence
\[
\max_{\sigma\in\{+,-\}}E_{P_\sigma}R_{P_\sigma}(\widehat\pi)
\ge \frac12 c_0 h_nP_X(B_n)
\ge c h_n^{1+\alpha}.
\]
Taking the infimum over all estimators in def:minimax-regret yields
\[
M_n(\alpha,\gamma)\ge c h_n^{1+\alpha}.
\]
Finally, by def:exponents, \(h_n=n^{-1/(2+\alpha+\beta_{\alpha,\gamma})}\), so
\[
h_n^{1+\alpha}=n^{-(1+\alpha)/(2+\alpha+\beta_{\alpha,\gamma})}=n^{-r_\star(\alpha,\gamma)}.
\]
This proves the minimax lower bound.

snapshot.stmt: Assume the two explicit witness-optimal policies of the corrected def:two-point-witness, namely x -> 1 and x -> 1{x notin B_n}, belong to Pi for all large n. Then M_n(alpha,gamma) = inf_{pi_hat} sup_{P in P_class} E_P R_P(pi_hat) >= c n^{-r_star(alpha,gamma)} for all large n, with c > 0 independent of n.

defs.def:minimax-regret: M_n(alpha,gamma) = inf over measurable data-dependent Pi-valued estimators pi_hat of sup_{P in P_class} E_P R_P(pi_hat).
defs.def:exponents: beta_ag = (0 if gamma = 0 else alpha gamma/(alpha+1)); D_ag = 2 + alpha + beta_ag; r_star(alpha,gamma) = (1+alpha)/D_ag.

oeq:feasible-upper.proof_tex:
We solve the open construction by choosing the clipping level from the additive drift--localized variance balance. Work conditionally on the training folds used to construct the cross-fitted nuisance estimates; on each held-out fold the score observations are independent of the fitted nuisance functions. Write \(\widehat e_q=\min\{1-q,\max\{q,\widehat e\}\}\), \(e_{0,q}=\min\{1-q,\max\{q,e_P\}\}\), and
\[
b_q(x)=E_P[\Gamma_q(O;\widehat\eta)\mid X=x]-\tau_P(x).
\]

First control the estimated-nuisance drift. By lem:clip-bias and the lower bound \(q\le \widehat e_q\le 1-q\),
\[
|b_q(x)|\le \frac{C}{q}|\widehat e_q(x)-e_P(x)|\{|\widehat\mu_1(x)-\mu_1(x)|+|\widehat\mu_0(x)-\mu_0(x)|\}.
\]
Taking expectation and applying Cauchy--Schwarz gives
\[
E_P|b_q(X)|\le \frac{C r_{\mu,n}}{q}\|\widehat e_q-e_P\|_{L_2(P_X)}. \tag{1}
\]
The clipping map is 1-Lipschitz, hence
\[
\|\widehat e_q-e_P\|_2\le \|\widehat e_q-e_{0,q}\|_2+\|e_{0,q}-e_P\|_2\le r_{e,n}+\|e_{0,q}-e_P\|_2 .
\]
For \(\gamma>0\), the oracle clipping displacement is supported on \(\{p_P\le q\}\) and is bounded by \(q\). Therefore ass:marginal-overlap implies
\[
\|e_{0,q}-e_P\|_2\le q\,P_X\{p_P\le q\}^{1/2}\le Cq^{1+1/(2\gamma)}.
\]
Substitution in (1) yields the global additive drift bound
\[
B_q:=\sup_{\pi\in\Pi}|P_X[(\pi-\pi_\star)b_q]|
\le E|b_q(X)|
\le C\left(\frac{r_{\mu,n}r_{e,n}}{q}+r_{\mu,n}q^{1/(2\gamma)}\right). \tag{2}
\]
This is deliberately not localized by the margin: with only separate \(L_2\) nuisance rates, the product term is controlled in \(L_1\), not as a localized \(L_2\) process. When \(\gamma=0\), choose a fixed clip \(q<\underline p\). Then \(e_{0,q}=e_P\), so (2) reduces to \(B_q\le C r_{\mu,n}r_{e,n}\).

Next control the centered empirical process. By lem:clipped-score-variance, conditionally on the training folds,
\[
\operatorname{Var}_P\{\Gamma_q(O;\widehat\eta)\mid X=x\}\le \frac{C}{\max\{p_P(x),q\}}\le \frac{C}{q}. \tag{3}
\]
Consequently, for any \(\pi\), with \(D_\pi=\{\pi\ne\pi_\star\}\),
\[
\| (\pi-\pi_\star)(\Gamma_q-E[\Gamma_q\mid X])\|_{L_2(P)}
\le C q^{-1/2}P_X(D_\pi)^{1/2}.
\]
By thm:margin-localization,
\[
P_X(D_\pi)^{1/2}\le C R_P(\pi)^{\alpha/(2+2\alpha)}. \tag{4}
\]
Applying ass:vc-localized-envelope to this finite-VC offset class and then using (4) gives, after the usual peeling over regret radii and up to a logarithmic factor,
\[
\left|(P_n-P)[(\pi-\pi_\star)\{\Gamma_q(O;\widehat\eta)-E(\Gamma_q\mid X)\}]\right|
\le C n^{-1/2}q^{-1/2}R_P(\pi)^{\alpha/(2+2\alpha)}(\log n)^{p_0} \tag{5}
\]
uniformly at the random ERM \(\widehat\pi_n\), in expectation after integrating the empirical-process event.

The ERM inequality \(P_n[\widehat\pi_n\Gamma_q]\ge P_n[\pi_\star\Gamma_q]\) gives
\[
R_P(\widehat\pi_n)
\le C n^{-1/2}q^{-1/2}R_P(\widehat\pi_n)^{\alpha/(2+2\alpha)}(\log n)^{p_0}+C B_q . \tag{6}
\]
Indeed, the population part equals \(-R_P(\widehat\pi_n)+P_X[(\widehat\pi_n-\pi_\star)b_q]\), and the drift is bounded by (2). Let \(\theta=\alpha/(2+2\alpha)\). Since \(1/(1-\theta)=2(1+\alpha)/(2+\alpha)\), (6) implies
\[
E_P R_P(\widehat\pi_n)
\le C\left(n^{-1/2}q^{-1/2}\right)^{2(1+\alpha)/(2+\alpha)}(\log n)^p+C B_q. \tag{7}
\]
This is the key point: only the stochastic term is passed through the margin self-bound; the nuisance drift remains additive.

Now assume \(\gamma>0\) and choose \(q=n^{-s}\wedge 1/4\). Let
\[
A_\alpha=\frac{1+\alpha}{2+\alpha}.
\]
Under ass:polynomial-nuisance-exponents, \(r_{\mu,n}=O(n^{-a})\) and \(r_{\mu,n}r_{e,n}=O(n^{-c})\). The three terms in (7) have exponents
\[
A_\alpha(1-s),\qquad c-s,
\qquad a+\frac{s}{2\gamma}.
\]
Therefore the best exponent delivered by the plain clipped-AIPW ERM is
\[
g(a,c,\gamma)=\sup_{s\ge0}\min\left\{A_\alpha(1-s),\ c-s,
\ a+\frac{s}{2\gamma}\right\}. \tag{8}
\]
By lem:clip-balance-exponent this equals the closed form
\[
g(a,c,\gamma)=\min\left\{A_\alpha,
 c,
 \frac{A_\alpha(1+2\gamma a)}{1+2\gamma A_\alpha},
 \frac{c+2\gamma a}{1+2\gamma}\right\}, \tag{9}
\]
and an optimizing clip exponent is \(s_\star=\max\{0,2\gamma(g-a)\}\), with \(q_n=n^{-s_\star}\wedge1/4\). Thus
\[
\sup_{P\in\mathcal P_{\alpha,\gamma}}E_P R_P(\widehat\pi_n)
\le C n^{-g(a,c,\gamma)}(\log n)^p.
\]
The reported feasible exponent is the information-capped exponent in def:feasible-rate,
\[
r_{\rm feas}(\alpha,\gamma,a,c)=\min\{r_\star(\alpha,\gamma),g(a,c,\gamma)\},
\]
so in particular \(r_{\rm feas}\le r_\star\), and the preceding display implies the asserted weaker bound with \(r_{\rm feas}\).

At \(\gamma=0\), choose fixed \(q<\underline p\). The stochastic term in (7) has exponent \(A_\alpha=(1+\alpha)/(2+\alpha)\), while the additive nuisance drift has exponent \(c\). Hence
\[
r_{\rm feas}(\alpha,0,a,c)=\min\left\{\frac{1+\alpha}{2+\alpha},c\right\}.
\]
Since \(\beta_{\alpha,0}=0\), \(r_\star(\alpha,0)=A_\alpha\); the strict-overlap endpoint attains the minimax exponent exactly when \(c\ge A_\alpha\).

snapshot.stmt: CONSTRUCT-AND-DETERMINE. Assume gamma<=alpha+1, ass:marginal-overlap, ass:nuisance-rate, ass:vc-localized-envelope, and at gamma=0 also ass:strict-overlap-endpoint. For the plain cross-fit clipped-AIPW ERM of def:feasible-erm with clip q chosen by the analysis, DERIVE (do NOT pre-specify) the best worst-case welfare-regret exponent r_feas with sup_{P in P_class(alpha,gamma)} E_P R_P(pi_hat_n) <= C n^{-r_feas} (log n)^p, by balancing the localized variance against the estimated-nuisance CLIP BIAS over q. State the clip q and the resulting r_feas in closed form, confirm r_feas<=r_star, and reduce at gamma=0.

defs.def:feasible-erm: Vhat_{n,q}(pi) = n^{-1} sum_i pi(X_i) Gamma_q(O_i; etahat^{(-k(i))}) with K-fold cross-fitted nuisances etahat and clip q in (0,1/2]; pi_hat_n in argmax_{pi in Pi} Vhat_{n,q}(pi). The clip q is the analysis tuning parameter (chosen by the bias-variance balance).
defs.def:clipped-aipw-score: Gamma_q(O; eta) = mu_1(X) - mu_0(X) + (A/e_q(X;eta))(Y - mu_1(X)) - ((1-A)/(1-e_q(X;eta)))(Y - mu_0(X)).
defs.def:feasible-rate: Let A_alpha=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha). For certified polynomial nuisance exponents a,c with r_mu_n=O(n^{-a}) and r_mu_n r_e_n=O(n^{-c}), c>=1/2: the clipped-AIPW ERM welfare regret is bounded by the GLOBAL (additive, NON-localized) estimated-nuisance drift r_mu_n r_e_n/q + r_mu_n q^{1/(2 gamma)} plus a localized stochastic term whose radius uses the AIPW conditional VARIANCE Var(Gamma_q|X) <~ 1/max{p,q} (scale n^{-1/2} q^{-1/2}), NOT the crude envelope q^{-1}. Balancing the clip q=n^{-s} gives g(a,c,gamma)=sup_{s>=0} min{A_alpha(1-s), c-s, a+s/(2 gamma)} and r_feas=min{r_star(alpha,gamma), g(a,c,gamma)}. At gamma=0 (strict-overlap floor, q fixed) r_feas=min{A_alpha, c}, which equals r_star=A_alpha when c>=A_alpha. The drift is GLOBAL because ||b_q||_2 cannot carry the r_mu r_e product from L2 bounds on each factor; only E|b_q| (Cauchy-Schwarz, L1) gives the product, and that enters regret additively, not through the margin self-bound.
defs.def:law-class: { P : ass:iid, ass:consistency, ass:unconfoundedness, ass:bounded-outcome, ass:positivity, ass:margin, ass:zero-effect, ass:overlap-decay, ass:marginal-overlap, ass:strict-overlap-endpoint, ass:policy-class, and ass:optimal-in-class hold at exponents (alpha,gamma) }

assumptions.ass:overlap-decay: P{p_P(X) <= v, 0 < |tau_P(X)| <= u} <= C_o u^alpha v^(1/gamma) for all 0 < v <= c_o u^gamma, with v^(1/gamma)=1 when gamma=0.
assumptions.ass:marginal-overlap: P{p_P(X) <= v} <= C_om v^(1/gamma) for all 0 < v <= c_o, with the convention v^(1/gamma)=1 when gamma=0.
assumptions.ass:nuisance-rate: The cross-fitted nuisance estimators satisfy ||muhat_a-mu_a||_{L2(P)} <= r_mu_n and ||ehat-e||_{L2(P)} <= r_e_n for a in {0,1}, with product-rate r_mu_n r_e_n = O(n^{-1/2}).
assumptions.ass:margin: P(0 < |tau_P(X)| <= u) <= C_m u^alpha for all 0 < u <= u_0.
assumptions.ass:vc-localized-envelope: For the finite-VC class Pi there is a localized Rademacher/Dudley entropy-integral envelope psi with psi(r) <= C sqrt(d_Pi/n) r polylog(n) controlling the localized offset empirical processes uniformly over the class.
assumptions.ass:strict-overlap-endpoint: When gamma=0, p_P(X) >= underline_p holds P_X-almost surely, for a constant underline_p in (0,1/2].
assumptions.ass:bounded-crossfit-nuisances: The cross-fitted outcome-regression estimates muhat_0, muhat_1 take values in [-1,1].
assumptions.ass:polynomial-nuisance-exponents: There exist a >= 0, c >= 1/2 and constants C_mu, C_prod such that r_mu_n <= C_mu n^(-a) and r_mu_n r_e_n <= C_prod n^(-c) for all large n.

thm:rate-characterization.proof_tex:
The lower bound is thm:minimax-lower: under the richness assumption that the two witness-optimal policies belong to \(\Pi\), the constructed laws lie in \(\mathcal P_{\alpha,\gamma}\), have bounded product divergence, and have regret separation \(h_n^{1+\alpha}\). Therefore
\[
M_n(\alpha,\gamma)\ge c n^{-r_\star(\alpha,\gamma)},
\qquad
r_\star(\alpha,\gamma)=\frac{1+\alpha}{2+\alpha+\beta_{\alpha,\gamma}}.
\]

For the upper side, use the explicit cross-fitted clipped-AIPW ERM of def:feasible-erm with the clip selected in oeq:feasible-upper. Since \(M_n\) is the infimum over all measurable \(\Pi\)-valued estimators, it is at most the risk of this estimator. The feasible-upper theorem gives
\[
\sup_{P\in\mathcal P_{\alpha,
\gamma}}E_PR_P(\widehat\pi_n)
\le C n^{-r_{\rm feas}(\alpha,\gamma,a,c)}(\log n)^p,
\]
where, for \(\gamma>0\),
\[
r_{\rm feas}=\min\{r_\star,g(a,c,\gamma)\},
\quad
 g(a,c,\gamma)=\sup_{s\ge0}\min\left\{A_\alpha(1-s),c-s,a+\frac{s}{2\gamma}\right\},
\]
with \(A_\alpha=(1+\alpha)/(2+\alpha)\), and where the equivalent closed form for \(g\) is supplied by lem:clip-balance-exponent. Thus
\[
M_n(\alpha,\gamma)
\le C n^{-r_{\rm feas}(\alpha,\gamma,a,c)}(\log n)^p.
\]
Combining the two displays proves the advertised two-sided bracket.

The phase comparison is now algebraic. Because \(r_{\rm feas}=\min\{r_\star,g\}\), one always has \(r_{\rm feas}\le r_\star\). Equality holds exactly on the branch \(g(a,c,\gamma)\ge r_\star(\alpha,\gamma)\). If \(g(a,c,\gamma)<r_\star(\alpha,\gamma)\), then \(r_{\rm feas}=g\) and the feasible upper exponent is strictly smaller than the information lower-bound exponent; this is precisely the nuisance-limited branch left for oeq:feasible-tight.

At \(\gamma=0\), oeq:feasible-upper gives
\[
r_{\rm feas}(\alpha,0,a,c)=\min\{A_\alpha,c\},
\qquad r_\star(\alpha,0)=A_\alpha.
\]
Therefore strict-overlap equality holds under the additional side condition \(c\ge A_\alpha\), and fails when the additive nuisance product rate is slower than the margin-driven stochastic exponent. This is the narrowed phase statement recorded in the proposed statement change; without the side condition \(c\ge A_\alpha\), the original unconditional equality-at-\(\gamma=0\) clause is too strong.

snapshot.stmt: Under ass:margin-window, ass:marginal-overlap, ass:nuisance-rate, the standing feasible-estimator conditions used in oeq:feasible-upper, Pi rich (witness-optimal policies in Pi), and gamma<=alpha+1: c n^{-r_star(alpha,gamma)} <= M_n(alpha,gamma) <= C n^{-r_feas(alpha,gamma)} (log n)^p, with r_star=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha+beta_{alpha,gamma}) and r_feas the derived plain-clipped-AIPW exponent. Moreover r_feas<=r_star, with equality at gamma=0 and more generally whenever kappa zeta(a,c,gamma)>=r_star(alpha,gamma). The strict positive gap occurs precisely in the nuisance-limited weak-overlap branch kappa zeta(a,c,gamma)<r_star(alpha,gamma); feasible tightness is resolved in the equal-exponent branch and remains open in the strict-gap branch (oeq:feasible-tight).

defs.def:feasible-rate: Let A_alpha=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha). For certified polynomial nuisance exponents a,c with r_mu_n=O(n^{-a}) and r_mu_n r_e_n=O(n^{-c}), c>=1/2: the clipped-AIPW ERM welfare regret is bounded by the GLOBAL (additive, NON-localized) estimated-nuisance drift r_mu_n r_e_n/q + r_mu_n q^{1/(2 gamma)} plus a localized stochastic term whose radius uses the AIPW conditional VARIANCE Var(Gamma_q|X) <~ 1/max{p,q} (scale n^{-1/2} q^{-1/2}), NOT the crude envelope q^{-1}. Balancing the clip q=n^{-s} gives g(a,c,gamma)=sup_{s>=0} min{A_alpha(1-s), c-s, a+s/(2 gamma)} and r_feas=min{r_star(alpha,gamma), g(a,c,gamma)}. At gamma=0 (strict-overlap floor, q fixed) r_feas=min{A_alpha, c}, which equals r_star=A_alpha when c>=A_alpha. The drift is GLOBAL because ||b_q||_2 cannot carry the r_mu r_e product from L2 bounds on each factor; only E|b_q| (Cauchy-Schwarz, L1) gives the product, and that enters regret additively, not through the margin self-bound.
defs.def:exponents: beta_ag = (0 if gamma = 0 else alpha gamma/(alpha+1)); D_ag = 2 + alpha + beta_ag; r_star(alpha,gamma) = (1+alpha)/D_ag.

lem:clip-bias.proof_tex:
Fix a measurable plug-in nuisance triple \(\bar\eta=(\bar\mu_0,\bar\mu_1,\bar e)\). Let \(\bar e_q(x)=\min\{1-q,\max\{q,\bar e(x)\}\}\) and \(\Delta_a(x)=\bar\mu_a(x)-\mu_a(x)\). Conditioning on \(X=x\), write \(e=e_P(x)\), \(\bar e_q=\bar e_q(x)\), \(\mu_a=\mu_a(x)\), and \(\bar\mu_a=\bar\mu_a(x)\). By def:clipped-aipw-score,
\[
E_P[\Gamma_q(O;\bar\eta)\mid X=x]
=\bar\mu_1-\bar\mu_0+\frac{e}{\bar e_q}(\mu_1-\bar\mu_1)-\frac{1-e}{1-\bar e_q}(\mu_0-\bar\mu_0).
\]
Subtracting \(\tau_P(x)=\mu_1-\mu_0\) gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
E_P[\Gamma_q(O;\bar\eta)\mid X=x]-\tau_P(x)
&=\Delta_1\left(1-\frac{e}{\bar e_q}\right)-\Delta_0\left(1-\frac{1-e}{1-\bar e_q}\right)\\
&=(\bar e_q-e)\left(\frac{\Delta_1}{\bar e_q}+\frac{\Delta_0}{1-\bar e_q}\right).
\end{aligned}
\]
This proves the displayed identity.

The identity also proves the stated non-cancellation claim. If \(\bar e_q=e_P\), the prefactor is zero; if \(\Delta_0=\Delta_1=0\), the bracket is zero. But \(p_P(x)>q\) only says that the true propensity is not at the clipping boundary. It does not imply \(\bar e_q(x)=e_P(x)\). For example, at a one-point covariate law with \(e_P=1/2\), \(q<1/4\), \(\bar e=1/2+\varepsilon\), and \(\Delta_1\ne0\), the true clipped region is empty but the drift equals \(\varepsilon\Delta_1/(1/2+\varepsilon)\ne0\). Hence no bound supported only on \(\{p_P\le q\}\) follows from the frozen assumptions alone.

snapshot.stmt:
Exact clipped-score drift identity. For any measurable plug-in nuisance triple \(\bar\eta=(\bar\mu_0,\bar\mu_1,\bar e)\), with \(\bar e_q=\min\{1-q,\max\{q,\bar e\}\}\) and \(\Delta_a=\bar\mu_a-\mu_a\), the conditional mean drift satisfies
\[
E_P[\Gamma_q(O;\bar\eta)\mid X=x]-\tau_P(x)
=(\bar e_q(x)-e_P(x))\left(\frac{\Delta_1(x)}{\bar e_q(x)}+\frac{\Delta_0(x)}{1-\bar e_q(x)}\right).
\]
Hence cancellation holds only where \(\bar e_q(x)=e_P(x)\) or both regression errors vanish; it does not follow merely from \(p_P(x)>q\). Under the frozen assumptions alone, no additive clipped-region bound of the form asserted in the current lemma is derivable.

defs.def:clipped-aipw-score: Gamma_q(O; eta) = mu_1(X) - mu_0(X) + (A/e_q(X;eta))(Y - mu_1(X)) - ((1-A)/(1-e_q(X;eta)))(Y - mu_0(X)).

oeq:feasible-tight.proof_tex: Exact feasible tightness is proved up to logarithms on the equal-exponent branch r_feas(alpha,gamma,a,c)=r_star(alpha,gamma): the cross-fitted clipped-AIPW ERM attains n^{-r_star}, and thm:minimax-lower rules out any faster polynomial exponent. At gamma=0 this requires and is equivalent to c>=A_alpha=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha). In the remaining strict-gap branch the strongest proved statement is c n^{-r_star(alpha,gamma)} <= M_n(alpha,gamma) <= C n^{-r_feas(alpha,gamma,a,c)}(log n)^p with r_feas<r_star; whether this bracket is tight remains the isolated open problem.

snapshot.stmt: OPEN: determine the welfare-regret rate attainable by a genuinely feasible estimator, in particular the cross-fitted clipped-AIPW ERM of def:feasible-erm, under weak overlap. Can any feasible estimator attain the converse exponent r_star(alpha,gamma), or must every feasible procedure be strictly slower because the weak-arm outcome regression must be learned on the clipped region?
